title: Extreme Heat Warning issued for Tuesday: EMS urges precautions as heat index reaches 110 to 116 - paxtonmedia.com

full_text:
As another heat wave approaches — and Canadian wildfire smoke enters the area — local professionals urge the community to take precautionary measures against the elements.
On Monday, Mercy Regional EMS’s Executive Director Jeremy Jeffrey highlighted the health risks associated with extreme heat, its impacts on the respiratory system and who is most vulnerable. WPSD Local 6’s Chief Meteorologist Trent Okerson added that the wildfire smoke impacts are not expected to be extreme in Paducah, but Jeffrey still offered preventative measures for those most at risk.
“Our body likes cool, moist air,” Jeffrey said. “When we get in that hot, humid air, we actually breathe faster. When we breathe faster, it causes the irritation in our lungs to increase, which leads to other things — especially for our patients in our community that have COPD, or asthmatics, or have heart conditions — because as our lungs get strained, so does our heart.”
Anticipating increased cases of heat exhaustion and heat stroke, Jeffrey added that he expects Tuesday to be a busy day for Mercy Regional EMS.
“Most of the individuals in our area have comorbidities such as COPD or asthma, and it's just going to exacerbate it and make it worse for them over the next couple of days,” Jeffrey said.
Alongside those who have chronic respiratory conditions, he said that smokers may also be at a higher risk of health issues as temperatures rise. Jeffrey explained that the respiratory system already works harder in high heat, and nicotine paralyzes cilia in the throat — the hairlike structures responsible for clearing mucus — which can exacerbate other symptoms.
“Smoking and vaping tomorrow would be bad,” Jeffrey said. “If we start getting poor air quality readings around here from the smoke that we know is coming, then it's only going to exacerbate it. It's going to make it worse.”
Some tips Jeffrey suggested for managing the incoming heat wave included staying inside and utilizing mist fans that emit cool, moist air. He also stressed the importance of hydrating before spending time outside in high heat.
“Start hydrating today. If you're going to be outside tomorrow, hydrate today. Electrolyte replenishment is huge,” Jeffrey said. “Once you get behind, there's no catching up — and then it's too late.”
As for the wildfire smoke predictions, Jeffrey compared the expected impacts to resemble those of springtime allergies — reactions that could lead to lung irritation and the exacerbation of illnesses like COPD and asthma. Simply staying inside, he added, may not offer much protection for those most susceptible to its effects.
“That smoke's going to make it into your residence,” he said. “The best thing you can do is keep your residence ventilated. Make sure you've got circulating air all throughout your house. HVAC units are great. Window units — not so much.”
For those living in apartments with window units, Jeffrey suggested using a fan to create more air circulation. He also reminded the community to keep emergency medications ready and accessible.
“Have that rescue inhaler close at all times. Carry it in your pocket, in your purse, in your backpack — whatever you may have with you — because when that hits, you want to get ahead of it,” Jeffrey said.
When drinking sports drinks for hydration, he said it is important to drink an equal amount of water to match it. In cases of shortness of breath, dizziness, altered levels of consciousness, fatigue or when a rescue inhaler does not help, Jeffrey recommended calling emergency services.
